text: The surgeon performs a three-port laparoscopic ileocolic resection, utilizing an umbilical entry with a twelve-millimeter port, a suprapubic twelve-millimeter port for extraction, and a five-millimeter port in the right lower quadrant.

The surgeon performs a three-port laparoscopic colectomy, utilizing an umbilical entry with a twelve-millimeter port, a suprapubic twelve-millimeter port for extraction, and a five-millimeter port in the right lower quadrant.
The camera focuses on the right lower quadrant, revealing the old hand-sewn anastomosis from a previous ileocolic resection.
The camera focuses on the pathology in the right lower quadrant, revealing the surgeon's previous hand-sewn anastomosis from the third ileocolic resection.
The surgical field is viewed through a multi-port laparoscopic approach, revealing minimal adhesions in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a multi-port laparoscopy, revealing a surgical field with minimal adhesions, attributed to the patient's immunosuppression.
The surgeon examines the small bowel and identifies a short segment stricture at the site of the previous ileocolic anastomosis. The surgical team prepares to mobilize this area for resection.

The surgeon has completed running the small bowel and identified a short segment stricture at the site of the previous ileocolic anastomosis. The surgical team begins mobilizing this area for resection.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes omental adhesions attached to the anastomosis site. Following this, the transverse colon is unfolded from the ascending colon, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the ileocolic resection.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes omental adhesions attached to the ileocolic anastomosis. Following this, the transverse colon is unfolded from the ascending colon, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon performs an extracorporeal anastomosis and extracts the bowel through the phanaceal incision, facilitated by the redundancy of the bowel.

The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the bowel, addressing the thick Crohn's mesentery by initially mobilizing laterally rather than taking the mesentery intracorporeally. The specimen is prepared for potential extracorporeal extraction for improved control.

The surgeon performs a lateral to medial mobilization of the bowel, opting for this approach due to the thick Crohn's mesentery, using a ligature for better control during the extracorporeal extraction of the specimen.
The surgeon mobilizes the area around the prior ileocolic anastomosis for resection, revealing well-preserved tissue planes despite multiple previous mobilizations.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the right colon, progressing towards the hepatic flexure. Care is taken to avoid injury to the ascending colon, as the plan is to preserve as much of the colon as possible. The mobilization continues from a lateral approach before transitioning to a superior view.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the right colon, progressing towards the hepatic flexure, carefully separating the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the hepatic flexure from a lateral approach, taking care to avoid injury to the ascending colon as the right colectomy is not planned.
The surgeon carefully dissects the area above the duodenum, ensuring not to cut too deeply during the initial burns and cuts. The duodenum is visible beneath the dissection site.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the duodenum, visible beneath the dissection area, using controlled burns and cuts. A sponge is placed to protect the duodenum during the procedure.
The surgeon places a sponge over the duodenum to protect it during the dissection process.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the bowel, ensuring there is no tension on the mesentery or bowel during extraction through the suprapubic port site, avoiding damage to non-specimen tissues.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the hepatic flexure, ensuring there is no tension on the mesentery or bowel during extraction through the suprapubic port site. The flexure is visibly lowered, and the reach is checked to ensure it extends well below the intended area.
The surgeon observes the hepatic flexure descending smoothly and checks its reach. An Alexis wound protector is upsized and positioned at the suprapubic location.
The surgeon identifies adhesions between the gallbladder and surrounding tissues, which require careful dissection to prevent complications.
The surgeon identifies adhesions to the gallbladder and proceeds to dissect them, facilitating the mobilization of the bowel for extracorporeal extraction.
The surgeon continues to take down the tissue, facilitating the mobilization of the bowel for extracorporeal extraction.

The surgeon uses a sponge to manage minor bleeding without cauterization or clipping. The mesentery is taken directly beneath the anastomosis, with the tissue being thin enough for easy handling in this position.

The surgeon uses a sponge to manage minor bleeding without cauterization or clipping. The mesentery is dissected directly beneath the anastomosis, with the tissue being thin enough for easy handling in this position.
The surgeon completes a side-to-side antiparastaltic anastomosis and checks the ICG fluorescence to assess perfusion. The mesenteric defects are left open, as is the surgeon's usual practice.
